summary: McKenzie recounts giving a presentation on data-driven software design to the Design Matters group at the Apple Store in Osaka. He adapted his originally technical presentation to suit a less geeky audience. He also met with MessaLiberty, a small Japanese company he found impressive, and did a video interview covering A/B testing, software design, and advice for working in Japan.
key_points: Adapting technical presentations for non-technical audiences requires retooling content to be less technical and more broadly interesting
MessaLiberty represents a hopeful model in Japan: a small team of young engineers working sane hours on meaningful products
A/B testing has diminishing returns and knowing when to redirect efforts elsewhere is as important as knowing when to test
money_quotes: I retooled the presentation into something less technical and more interesting on the same topic.
Incidentally, that Slideshare widget is great SEO now isn't it. I'm leaving their links attached out of sheer amusement.
All with a team of about seven young engineers working sane hours. Ah, there is hope for the future yet.

Topics include a brief overview of the above presentation, when you should start A/B testing versus when to redirect your efforts elsewhere, and my advice for getting a job in Japan (spoiler: learn Japanese).
key_insight: Using data and statistics to drive software design decisions is valuable, but knowing when to apply A/B testing versus redirecting effort elsewhere is equally important.
